QF.id: Securities Types Features and Asset Allocation Concepts_34

question.text: Yield to Maturity (YTM) of a bond is defined as:

answer.text: The discount rate that equates the present value of all future cash flows from the bond with its current market price
The annual coupon payment divided by the face value of the bond
The total dividends paid by the bond issuer over the life of the bond
The difference between the face value and the issue price of the bond

explanation.text:
**The Logic:** YTM is the rate which equates the present value of future cash flows from a bond with the current price of the bond. As bond price changes, YTM also changes. YTM is the discount rate implied in the bond value at a point in time and is the most widely used method for computing return on bond investment.
**The Trap:** Coupon divided by face value (option B) is the coupon rate, not YTM. Dividends (option C) apply to equity, not bonds. Face value minus issue price (option D) is relevant only for zero coupon bonds and is not YTM.
**Cross-Exam Context:** Foundation topic tested across 22 NISM exams. YTM is the standard yield measure in debt markets, tested in NISM XII and all bond-related certifications.
**Distributor's Pro-Tip:** When comparing different bonds or debt funds, always look at YTM rather than coupon rate. A bond with 8% coupon trading at premium will have YTM below 8%, while one trading at discount will have YTM above the coupon. YTM is the true expected return.
